summary: Joel Spolsky introduces the concept of task allocation in software team management, arguing it's a critical skill that managers must master. He uses the Hebrew colloquialism 'file dumping' to describe assigning work to people. The post serves as a teaser for his longer article 'Human Task Switches Considered Harmful,' which argues that programmers should only work on one task at a time. He contends that proper task allocation can yield incredible productivity benefits, while poor allocation leads to organizational paralysis.
key_points: Task allocation is one of the first and most important skills for managers of programmers to master
Assigning work correctly can yield incredible productivity benefits; doing it wrong creates organizational dysfunction where nothing gets done
Each programmer should only be working on one thing at a time to avoid harmful context switching
money_quotes: When you're managing a team of programmers, one of the first things you have to learn to get right is task allocation.
How you decide which files to dump in which laps is one of the areas where you can get incredible productivity benefits if you do it right.
Do it wrong, and you can create one of those gnarly situations where nobody gets anything accomplished and everybody complains that 'nothing ever gets done around here.'
key_insight: Programmers should work on only one task at a time, because poor task allocation and context switching destroy team productivity far more than most managers realize.
